--- author: - name: "Christina Muedsam" date: last-modified number-sections: false execute: warning: false message: false --- # Project Hail Mary {.unnumbered} *DNAzyme-like motifs, horizontal gene transfer, and short linear motif conservation in Potyviridae* [⚑ preprint — not peer reviewed]{.preprint-badge} ::: {.column-margin} **⚑ Preprint — not peer reviewed** This collection is under active development and has not been peer reviewed. Much of it is exploratory, and several of the central ideas (in particular the DNAzyme-activity, horizontal-gene-transfer, and AP-4 / tyrosine-motif immune hypotheses) are presented as explicit, testable hypotheses rather than established results — they are flagged as such throughout. Analyses, figures, interpretations and conclusions may change; please treat everything here as a living draft and cite with that caveat. Feel free to contribute. Constructive feedback is always welcome (no matter who you are or what your background is!). ::: ### Explore by thread The work spans multiple interconnected research threads, each running through several chapters. Drag a node to rearrange, hover to trace its connections, click a chapter to open it. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive map of five analysis threads and the chapters that investigate them. Drag to rearrange, hover to trace connections, click a chapter node to open it. ::: ::: Prefer a plain list? The chapter table below covers the same ground. The work spans several interconnected research threads: | Chapter | Topic | Key tools | |---|---|---| | [1 — Discovery](notebooks/01_discovery.qmd) | DNAzyme match + eukaryotic homologs | BLAST, Biostrings | | [2 — Phylogenetics](notebooks/02_phylogenetics.qmd) | CI protein tree, motif mapping | MAFFT, IQ-TREE 2, ape | | [3 — Codon Usage](notebooks/03_codon_dnazyme.qmd) | RSCU, ENC, host adaptation, DNAzyme locus | potytools, seqinr | | [4 — ELM Motifs](notebooks/04_protein_motifs.qmd) | Short linear motif conservation in CI | ELM REST API, potytools | | [5 — Potyvirus Motifs](notebooks/05_potyvirus_motifs.qmd) | CI 3D structure, motif catalogue, interaction network | AlphaFold, igraph, visNetwork | | [6 — HGT](notebooks/06_hgt.qmd) | Phylogenetic incongruence, sequence logos | ggseqlogo, potytools | | [7 — Networks](notebooks/07_networks.qmd) | Host-virus ecological networks | igraph, BioNexus app | | [8 — Immunity](notebooks/08_immu_simmu.qmd) | PTI/ETI simulation under genetic perturbations | Observable JS | | [9 — AP-4 Trafficking](notebooks/09_ap4_trafficking.qmd) | AP-4/tyrosine-motif immune-trafficking hypothesis | — | ## Lessons learned - A DNAzyme catalytic domain sequence matches perfectly to a region in the genomes of several potyviruses (6 species, 16 isolates). - The flanking regions of those DNAzyme-matching sequences (16 isolates, 9 identical by sequence) are identical to regions in the genomes of several eukaryotes, including plants (potential viral hosts) and insects (potential viral vectors). - Interactions between those eukaryotic species suggest the homology might reflect cross-kingdom horizontal DNA transfer. - The eukaryotic genes span diverse functions — DNA repair, transcription regulation, chromatin remodelling, RNA processing, translation, and genes of unknown function — several of them essential for cell survival and development. - The CI protein also carries conserved short linear motifs (ELM) beyond the DNAzyme-matching sequence itself, including an accessible tyrosine-based (YXXΦ) sorting signal alongside an acidic-dileucine AP-2 motif, recurring with genus-specific variation across isolates. - Those sorting motifs motivate a testable hypothesis that the CI protein co-opts host intracellular trafficking — potentially via the AP-4 adaptor complex — to aid viral replication and spread, and to perturb the same trafficking machinery used in plant immune signalling. ### Reproducibility All computationally intensive steps (alignment, phylogenetic inference, NCBI fetching, ELM queries) are orchestrated by a `targets` pipeline: ```r # Install the pipeline dependencies once install.packages(c("targets", "tarchetypes")) remotes::install_local("packages/potytools") # Run the full pipeline (skips up-to-date steps automatically) targets::tar_make() # Visualise dependencies targets::tar_visnetwork() ``` External tools required on `PATH` for running the entire pipeline: `mafft`, `iqtree2`, `hmmscan`. ## Interactive Companion App The **BioNexus** Shiny app provides live GBIF + GLOBI biodiversity network exploration relevant to Chapters 7 and 8: ```r shiny::runApp("shiny/bionexus/") ``` ## Package Analysis functions are collected in the **potytools** R package (`packages/potytools/`), being prepared for rOpenSci peer review. ## Project History | Date | Milestone | |---|---| | Feb 2026 | DNAzyme match first identified (`2025_toys/poty.qmd`) | | Mar 2026 | HGT analysis suite | | Apr 2026 | Codon usage analysis iterations | | May 2026 | Cleaned codon + DNAzyme notebook | | May 2026 | Final phylogenetic pipeline | | Jun 2026 | ELM motif & immunity notebooks; this document | ## Overview This collection documents the discovery and characterisation of a DNAzyme-like catalytic domain sequence that maps precisely to the genomes of multiple Potyviridae species, with flanking regions matching eukaryotic genes across plant, fungal, and animal kingdoms. Cross-kingdom horizontal gene transfer is a possible explanation and presence-absence analyses of the potentially viral fragment in related plant species support the idea. However, considering that activity-critical positions show substitutions in homologous Potyvirus species, DNAzyme activity due to the presence of the detected sequence, in vivo, and especially DNAzymatic activity of homologous Potyvirus sequences, is still unlikely or a very rare event - and would additionally require conciding reverse transcriptase activity in the host. Additionally, the Potyviridae DNAzyme-like sequence, corresponds to a conserved protein motif in the viral CI helicase. Motif homology and the CIs' subcellular distribution and regulation suggest that the tyrosine-based motif could be involved in viral-host interactions associated with intracellular sorting (potentially via AP-4), viral replication, viral spread and immunity. The motif is conserved across species and kingdoms and known to be involved in and crucial for processes such as autophagy and endosomal processing and correct targeting. ### Long-shot hypothesis I further suspect that i) defects in components orchestrating protein sorting, recycling and degradation, or ii) viral particle overload (e.g. during infections) could result in endosomal jamming, lipid distortions and sporadic reversal of membrane (incl. receptor or transporter) topologies due to insufficient coordination at the late endosome. I also suspect that this mechanism applies not only to the plant system. Motif and pathway conservation instead suggest that it might be conserved in multicellular organisms. ### Side-quests and follow-ups - if the presence of viral sequences in plants (or insects) indeed turns out to be originating from HGT, it's another evidence that many of such events are still remaining undetected. - DNAzyme activity might not occur on its own in biological systems, but did anyone ever try to turn it around and fight potyviruses with DNAzymes?! One of the limiting factors in medicine might not be limiting in plants (presence of magnesium-ions) - one of the annoying but important questions was "ssDNA in a living cell? When is there ssDNA in a cell, at all?!". And the answer to that brings me to one of the few branches of the tree of life that are otherwise excluded in this document: bacteria. Specifically: Agrobacteria which transmit part of their plasmid DNA into plant cells as ssDNA.
